title: The First Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: February 21, 2021

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the First Sunday in Lent, the opening Sunday of the 40-day penitential season leading to Easter (which fell on April 4 in 2021). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have been Genesis 2:15-17, 3:1-7 (the temptation of Adam and Eve); Psalm 32; Romans 5:12-19 (Paul on sin and grace through Christ); and Matthew 4:1-11 (Jesus's temptation in the wilderness). The liturgical color is purple, and many Episcopal parishes would be observing fasting practices or spiritual disciplines during this opening week of Lent.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early stages of pandemic recovery, with many dioceses still operating under modified worship protocols established in 2020. The Diocese of Colorado (which oversees Saint John's Cathedral in the Denver metropolitan area) had been gradually reopening in-person services with capacity limits and safety measures. The national church was preparing for its 80th General Convention, scheduled for July 2021 in Baltimore, with pre-convention discussions underway regarding pandemic response and racial justice initiatives.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing winter weather typical for late February, with the city recently emerging from a significant winter storm that had hit the region in mid-February. Local politics focused on the ongoing impact of COVID-19 on Denver's economy and the city's vaccine rollout, which was accelerating in late February. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, where the Cathedral sits, was showing signs of recovery from pandemic closures, though many businesses remained under capacity restrictions.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Senate had just concluded the second impeachment trial of former President Donald Trump on February 13, acquitting him with 57 votes for conviction (10 short of the 67 needed). The nation was in the midst of its vaccine rollout with over 48 million doses administered by late February, though supply constraints and distribution inequality were major concerns. The economy was beginning to show signs of recovery with job growth returning, though unemployment remained elevated at 6.2%, and Congress was debating President Biden's $1.9 trillion COVID relief package (American Rescue Plan), which would pass the Senate on March 6.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Myanmar had been convulsed by a military coup on February 1, 2021, with the military deposing elected leader Aung San Suu Kyi and sparking massive pro-democracy protests and a violent crackdown. The global death toll from COVID-19 had exceeded 2.4 million, with Brazil and India becoming major hotspots as variant strains emerged. The Vatican was preparing for Pope Francis's upcoming trip to Iraq (scheduled for March), which would be his first international travel since the pandemic began.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture remained profoundly shaped by pandemic fatigue and division, with vaccination becoming an increasingly politicized issue. The nation was marking one year since COVID-19 shutdowns began, creating a reflective and somber mood alongside hope generated by vaccine distribution. The Super Bowl had taken place just one week prior (February 7), with limited attendance at Tampa Bay, symbolizing the strange new normal; culturally, there was growing awareness of racial justice issues following the murder of George Floyd, with the trial of Derek Chauvin set to begin on March 8.
